data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Strong Growth Acceleration
data.description:
Article Thesis

Palo Alto Networks (PANW) is a growth company that has recently seen its shares come under a lot of pressure. While shares are still far from a bargain, they are cheaper than they were in the past -- potentially making them attractive for investors with a longer-term view that want exposure to the fast-growing cybersecurity market and that do not mind the company's share-based compensation expenses.

Past Coverage

I have written about Palo Alto Networks here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in November, when I published this article. I was neutral on the stock back then, noting its growth potential but also its relatively high valuation. Shares have pulled back over the last three months, and with Palo Alto Networks having announced its earnings results a couple of days ago, I want to update my views on the company today.

What Happened?

Palo Alto Networks reported its most recent earnings results, for the company's fiscal second quarter, a couple of days ago. These were the headline results:

We see that the company delivered a double beat versus the analyst consensus estimate, which was, of course, a nice surprise. The revenue beat was pretty minor, at less than 1%, but Palo Alto Networks outperformed the bottom line estimate by around 10%, which is a pretty wide margin.

Palo Alto Networks: Recent Results Review

The reported revenue growth rate of Palo Alto Networks during its fiscal Q2 was pretty nice in absolute terms, at a little less than 15%, but the growth rate was slightly lower compared to the average over the last couple of quarters -- during the previous three quarters, PANW's reported revenue growth was a little above 15%. While revenue growth deceleration isn't great, it also isn't a disaster, I believe -- it was pretty minor, and the comparison to the previous year's second quarter wasn't easy. Reported revenue growth also only tells one part of the story when it comes to underlying business growth -- looking at other metrics is important, too. One such metric to watch is remaining performance obligations, or RPO. Here, Palo Alto Networks showed very appealing growth, with RPO rising by 23% versus one year earlier -- a much higher growth rate compared to the company's reported revenues.

This means that PANW's "backlog" of future revenue is expanding nicely, which should result in nice revenue growth in the coming years. The businesses that Palo Alto Networks' management sums up under Next Generation Security have shown a pretty strong growth rate during the period, with annual recurring revenue for this part of the company's business growing by 33% from one year earlier. With annual recurring revenue of more than $6 billion, this part of the business now generates more than half of the company's overall revenue -- when the fastest-growing business of a company grows bigger and bigger in terms of overall revenue contribution, there's potential for the overall business growth rate to get a lift. Analysts seem to believe that this is exactly what will play out, as the revenue forecast for this year and next year is considerably above what we saw in recent months: Wall Street analysts believe that Palo Alto Networks will grow its revenues by 22% this year and by another 20% next year, with this growth being largely driven by the company's Next Generation Security offerings.

The strong growth that is being forecasted by analysts is also reflected in the company's guidance for the current year. Management believes that annual recurring revenue for the Next Generation Security business will rise to around $7.95 billion during the current quarter, fiscal Q3, which would represent growth of around 56% compared to one year earlier -- that's not only a very attractive growth rate all by itself, but also means substantial growth acceleration versus what we saw during the most recent quarter. I think that this is a very good sign for the near term, as growth momentum is, at least according to management's outlook, on the company's side. The same holds true when we look at the full-year outlook, as the company's management sees ARR for its high-growth businesses grow at a mid-50s pace, relative to one year earlier, with ARR ending the year at more than $8.5 billion.

When we look at profitability, we see that Palo Alto Networks' net income grew nicely during the most recent quarter, with net earnings growth coming in a lot higher than revenue growth, thanks to considerable margin expansion. This was the result of operating expenses growing less compared to revenues and gross profits -- not too surprising, as many software companies benefit from operating leverage. Developing new software features costs a certain amount of cash, but development costs don't depend on the number of users of this software, and the same holds true for administrative expenses, etc. During the most recent quarter, for example, R&D expenses were up by just 1% year-over-year, despite annual recurring revenue and GAAP revenue growing a lot more. So as the company grows, its margins should grow over time as well, unless something unforeseen happens.

Palo Alto Networks tends to have, like many other fast-growing tech companies, a considerable lag between its GAAP profits and its adjusted profits, due to the company excluding a lot of share-based compensation expenses from its adjusted profits. Adjusted profits were roughly twice as high as GAAP profits—share-based compensation totaled $320 million during the most recent quarter alone. While this number was down slightly compared to one year earlier, it still resulted in considerable dilution. While many tech companies exclude share-based compensation from their non-GAAP results, I think this is somewhat questionable: This isn't a cash item, but it comes at a real cost to shareholders, so investors should not completely ignore the substantial pace of shares being issued to employees and management.

Is Palo Alto Networks A Good Investment?

Palo Alto Networks offers exposure to the fast-growing cybersecurity market, and its growth outlook for both the current quarter and the remainder of the year is quite good. Its next-generation businesses are growing quickly, with annual recurring revenue being forecast to grow by more than 50% this quarter. Operating leverage works in the company's favor, with PANW benefiting from nice margin expansion tailwinds.

I do believe that share-based compensation remains an issue, however, and when we look at Palo Alto Networks' GAAP results, profitability isn't overly strong, despite considerable margin growth over the last year. PANW trades at around 40x this year's non-GAAP earnings, but its GAAP earnings multiple is way higher than that -- depending on where you stand on the question of how investors should treat share-based compensation expenses, you may find Palo Alto Networks quite pricey. The stock has come down in recent months and is now less expensive than a quarter ago, but that doesn't make the stock cheap -- I thus remain on the sidelines for now.

For an investor who sees PANW's share issuance to employees more like an investment than a cost, PANW could look more appealing, though. At the current price and valuation, PANW is far from a strong buy, I believe, but a potential buy for those who want growth, cyber exposure, and who do not mind the company's share dilution too much.

data.title: Horizon Technology Finance: When A 20% Yield Is Not A Bargain But A Warning
data.description:
Introduction

Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) looks attractively inexpensive, but in reality it is cheap for a reason. Its venture-focused lending model worked in the zero-rate era, when there was a lot of capital in the market, and funders could exit easily. That environment no longer exists.

HRZN’s earnings and growth have gone down. The stock trades at roughly 0.9x book, and about 5–6x earnings. The stress is visible in the share price, which is down 30%. It is now pursuing a merger in an effort to stabilize scale and costs. However, I am skeptical that this will be enough. HRZN’s 20% yield may look attractive, but it reflects elevated structural risk rather than opportunity.

The Model That Broke For HRZN

HRZN is a BDC (a business development company) that does venture lending, offering structured debt to VC-backed technology and life sciences companies. HRZN does this at an earlier stage than traditional middle-market BDCs. These underwrite recurring cash flow. HRZN underwrites growth and the option to exit quickly. Here, repayment does not often come from cash flow but from follow-on equity raises, acquisitions, or public offerings.

There was abundant capital during the pre-2022 zero-rate period, and refinancing came easily. At that time, HRZN grew its portfolio and earnings. The past decade’s data shows this growth - revenue compounded 12.73% annually, EBIT grew at 15.74%. However, as rates rose and IPO markets faltered in 2022, venture lending as a whole suffered, and HRZN was severely impacted. In the recent past, revenue is down 5.41% YoY, net income margin is negative at -24.19%, and ROE is down 7.27%. Projections are even more dire, with forward EPS projections down 19.51%.

This is not short term volatility. The problem is more structural, and reflects a lending model exposed to a tighter capital environment that does not show any indication of being temporary.

Dividend Pressure, Coverage, And Balance Sheet Strain

HRZN’s forward dividend yield is an attractive-looking 20.47%. However, the payout ratio of 116.81% is concerning - a BDC that pays more than it earns is not operating from a position of strength. That is why dividend safety is graded D+, and consistency is C-. Those are not reassuring for an income vehicle selling itself on yield.

In Q3, management reported net investment income of $0.32 per share, modestly covering the $0.11 monthly distribution for the time being. However, the coverage cushion, at ~97%, is thin, so HRZN needs to keep charging higher interest rates to maintain earnings. However, as management itself says, that yield, at around 18.6% in Q3, is unusually high. Normalized historical yield is closer to 14.5-15%. That higher yield is a result of riskier credit, and is not sustainable. On the other hand, if the rate falls but volume still remains low due to tighter credit conditions, that would compress net interest income.

Adding to that problem is a difficult balance sheet. The net investment portfolio declined to $585 million from $636 million sequentially. Leverage is currently at 0.94x, lower than the target of 1.2-1.3x. Available liquidity stood at $151 million.

That lower leverage reduces immediate risk, but it also shows that the portfolio is contracting. Management expects originations to exceed prepayments to move leverage back toward target levels. However, that effectively increases risk exposure in a difficult venture market that remains sensitive to liquidity.

For HRZN, bad news keeps coming, with funding costs rising as well. In December, HRZN priced $57.5 million of 7.00% notes due 2028 to refinance 4.875% notes due 2026. So they are replacing sub-5% debt with 7% debt, that too in a stressed earnings environment that only increases interest expense pressure.

Take them all together - elevated payout ratio, negative profitability metrics, high, unsustainable portfolio yields, and rising funding costs—and the bear picture becomes clearer. That 20% yield does not appear as attractive as before. It doesn’t reflect excess earnings; it is simply the price the company is forced to pay investors to put their money in a higher risk asset.

The MRCC Merger - Stabilization, Not Transformation

Given these problems, HRZN is planning a merger with Monroe Capital Corporation (MRCC). The plan is to increase scale, reduce expense ratios, and improve efficiency. Management expects annual G&A savings of roughly $2.5 million, or about a 30% reduction. There will be fee waivers totaling up to $4 million in the first year after the deal is closed. These cost improvements are meaningful.

That said, the merger does not change the underlying business model - the one that was falling apart. HRZN will still remain a venture-focused lender. It will operate in the same stressed and liquidity-sensitive segment. Scale will do nothing to change those structures. It may smooth volatility, especially by making expenses a smaller percentage of the business, but it is not going to completely eliminate concentration risk. The stressed capital market conditions will also remain the same, and their effect on the company will not reduce.

In that sense, the merger appears defensive rather than transformational.

Peer Comparison: Venture Exposure vs. Diversified Scale

Among HRZN’s venture-lending peers are Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Trinity Capital (TRIN). While these are better than HRZN in many ways, they are still highly exposed to the venture scene, which is stressed right now. So if you need to invest in BDCs, you may also consider larger, diversified middle-market platforms like Ares Capital (ARCC) and Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX). Here’s a comparison table:

Here, there's a considerable difference in size, which improves scale efficiency in many ways - something that HRZN is trying to achieve with the MRCC merger. Access to deal flows, better ability to absorb quick credit losses are some of the other advantages. However, as against that, there is the problem with the venture-backed sector itself, which cannot be surmounted by just a merger.

Again, yield does not mean quality. HRZN’s portfolio yield is the highest, but that comes with a negative ROE and higher realized losses. Both ARCC and TSLX come with much lower portfolio yields, yet produce consistent double-digit ROEs and stable dividend coverage. HTGC and TRIN do show that the venture model can work, but needs better underwriting and broader origination.

The takeaway is that venture lending is not inherently flawed, but that the tight conditions of recent times, coupled with possibly better business management at some of the peers, have helped them outclass HRZN.

Upcoming Earnings: What to Watch

With earnings due next month, we need to watch for improvements in dividend coverage. NII relative to the $0.11 monthly distribution will tell us if the payout remains well-covered or we should worry about earnings slipping further away from it.

Investors need to watch for portfolio yield and leverage as well. If yields normalize but origination volume doesn’t improve, earnings could compress. More than raw growth, I would watch for the credit quality of new loans so there are fewer non-accruals.

Apart from non-accruals, funding costs and NAV movement will determine balance sheet stress. Next month’s earnings will show if HRZN may be truly turning around, or simply plowing through ongoing structural pressure.

Conclusion

HRZN hasn’t been declining because of some temporary volatility or any other resolvable cause. The decline reflects a venture-focused lending business designed to work well in a liquidity-rich environment; unfortunately for HRZN, that environment has now significantly tightened.

HRZN’s earnings have weakened, and the dividend is not well-covered by what it earns. It is forced to use higher interest rates, which elevates portfolio yield above sustainable historical norms. As rates rise, funding costs are rising. As I also noted, HRZN needs to expand risk exposure so it can restore its targets for leverage.

None of these items suggest signs of durable improvement.

At this time, the company is pursuing a merger with MRCC in the hopes of redressing the situation. It will, indeed, improve scale and cost efficiencies; however, it doesn’t change the business model, nor the problems associated with it. Current capital markets are simply not like what they were five years ago, and HRZN has not adapted its business to the new conditions.

So, a 20% dividend yield may look compelling, but it is just the price they are paying you to accept their risk. It is not something being paid because they have so much earnings. On top of that, there are much better BDCs - both the venture-focused ones, and those targeting middle and upper markets - that make far better sense than HRZN. As such, it is an avoid.

data.title: Nvidia Will Likely Beat, But The Market May Not Like It
data.description:
I was on the Investing Experts Podcast this week, and, as with any time a group of young analysts meet, we talked about Nvidia Corp. (NVDA). Not only is it still the world's largest firm after the tech sector corrected this year, although it's now below the $5T market cap mark.

It had a steep drawdown at the beginning of the month, but has snapped back and is now flat YTD.

My record on NVDA is short, as I mostly covered it through funds that give exposure to NVDA like the YieldMax NVDY ETF, having started coverage of the stock itself in July. I've maintained a buy rating through today.

It's up nearly 10% since I first recommended it, but so is the S&P 500.

Here's my rating history:

The Pressure of High Expectations

In that conversation, there were a couple of things said that I think are worth bringing up in an article directly on Nvidia, especially considering that they have their earnings report next week, on the 25th.

I'll focus on just one here. My colleague Julia Ostian said on the podcast about Nvidia's earnings:

The market looks nervous and I mean the market looks excited but worried at the same time...I just broke down Nvidia's earnings today, I wrote the preview...

It really looks like it doesn't matter what Nvidia will do. The market will not be as happy because it looks to me that the company so many times has beaten the expectations that basically right now the expectations are that everything will be better than expected.

But the problem is that this phrase can stretch basically limitless, right? What is better than expected?

...if Nvidia will deliver instead of $65 billion, $70 billion, I'm not sure the market will be very happy because Nvidia usually beats like three to four billion.

...I really think the stock can go lower on the earnings even with the [beat].

As I began to write this article, my take on NVDA earnings, I kept coming back to that line, that expectations of beating expectations can be dangerous for sentiment. Sentiment is one of the key mechanisms for earnings calls—sentiment changes bring volume, and that brings volatility. It can spiral quickly.

This isn't a new problem for NVDA, based on the last two years. We can see that when earnings estimates and reality meet or beat, they sell off just like when they miss. As some commenters have said about this phenomenon in one of my articles, “NVDA dropping on earnings is a meme.”

For reference, the “E” symbols denote an earnings call.

It has to do with these high expectations. Beats don't always impress the market, and so good news often leads to pain even if the overall trajectory is still up. While NVDA rarely saw stock gains immediately after earnings, that 2-year gain is still ~280%.

That is absolute insanity for companies this large, but the same goes for the earnings and revenue growth, which is why the market is this way about NVDA. When your EBITDA looks like this, the market has sky-high expectations, and for many traders, it's unclear what that really means.

Those who believe the meme will understand one of my past headlines: “No earnings call would've been good enough.”

NVDA Q4 Preview

As shown earlier, the Wall St. consensus for NVDA is near $70B in revenue for Q4. Corporate guidance has the headline at $65B, with gross margins at ~74%. EPS guidance is at ~$1.50, which would be a 71% YoY increase, but Wall St. consensus is even higher.

The last quarter read on gross margins was a little under target, and improvement in margins is one of the things I believe the market cares about with NVDA. I watch this figure closely, despite how high its absolute level is.

The market seems to respond to changes in it, although overlay a macro lens on this chart and maybe you don't see what I see—I can't tell if I have my blinders on here or if this looks how I think it looks.

Combine that with my ongoing push of profitability theory, the name I've given the thesis that index valuations can be tied to profitability. NVDA is the king of this class of businesses that have built very high profit margins and thus produce immense free cash flow growth.

NVDA did $22B in FCF last quarter, up ~98% YoY, with guidance at ~$25B for this quarter, unlikely but potentially a new all-time high if they overshoot—which, as mentioned before, is expected by Mr. Market.

Back to margins, I expect commentary in the earnings call itself will matter here, as investors want to hear that Blackwell chip sales are holding their margins as production scales. These are some of the newer chips that are being sold as part of the cyclical dealings that some investors are skeptical of.

Rubin may be a sore topic, as it has been a high cost with little reward yet, but that is because it is still early in the production cycle. Since NVDA is primarily a design firm and not a manufacturer, they spend a lot on R&D, especially early in the development cycle.

Critics have rightfully pointed out rising R&D costs.

But R&D is a relatively fixed cost for GPU development cycles, whereas hyperscaler capex is dynamic and now approaching $1T. As their revenue has exploded in the past two years, the ratio has dropped precipitously.

I see this increase in R&D spending by NVDA to be opportunistic, especially because even as it has doubled, its impact on bottom lines has halved. They just can afford to spend more because they make more.

In the call itself, I expect that the TPU vs. GPU conversation may come up (competition with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL)), which is really a question about sustained data center demand, or if that will fall off as they transition to a different kind of chip. I have no speculation on how Huang will answer that, but if he isn't confident and backs that up with a technical point for GPUs, that could rattle investor confidence.

One thing we can expect is volatility. Options are currently pricing in an ~11% move in either direction. It's a little crazy to consider, in liquidity terms, that a move of that magnitude means hundreds of billions made or lost for investors.

The big risk outside what I've already mentioned is that forward guidance is down. Because of all the CapEx announcements from hyperscalers hitting headlines lately, investors want to see that NVDA is capitalizing on that and providing the supply. If guidance is down, or anyone in the call mentioned spending fatigue, that could cause a sentiment panic quickly, and a sell-off, even if it rebounds the next day.

An 11% implied options move doesn't mean it will stay up or down 11% if it moves that far, but that we may see that move at a peak. Often, NVDA has rebound days following large moves, so watch out for that if you are trading around the earnings call.

Conclusion

I am maintaining my buy rating on NVDA, as it continues to be a powerhouse company in all the places that matter, like its gross margins, free cash flow, and marginal cost of development. They are an exemplary business and highly valued for good reason.

They are a firm that is often “priced to perfection” and punished for not beating estimates higher than their guidance. That's why they have a history of selling off after good earnings, as they may very well do next week.

If they do, I will be buying more shares.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Apple: Why I'm Finally Turning Bullish (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Last fall, I reiterated my hold rating on Apple Inc. (AAPL) as I cited solid results, a justified valuation, and a lack of catalysts. My neutral stance has held up well, with the stock having shown modest losses since the publication of that article. Apple's most recent earnings report a few weeks ago made headlines as iPhone sales were reported to be strong, particularly in China. Today, we'll review these results as well as discuss some noteworthy developments to see if Apple has finally become a buy in my eyes.

Below, it is shown that FY2026 Q1 was a strong quarter for Apple. Growth has accelerated with iPhone sales and the Chinese market powering their results. Looking forward, their financial guidance is solid, and for the long term, higher R&D spending combined with increased AI efforts shows that the company is moving back to its innovation roots. While there are some risks in the form of memory shortages, I would say that the strengthening momentum of their business shows there is room for multiple expansions from here. Therefore, I'm finally turning bullish on Apple stock.

Business Is Good

My first impression of Apple's top-line results is that business was good in their FY2026 Q1. Their products and services segments generated combined revenues of $143.76 billion, and that represented a strong growth rate of 15.65% YoY. You can see above that this was one of the largest accelerations in growth in the past few years and that the growth rate in the first quarter was the strongest in a long time. Therefore, Apple's overall business activity seems to be picking up and is seeing strong momentum.

It is also worth mentioning that the company scored a breathtaking beat of expectations. In Q1, their top line surpassed analyst estimates by a shocking $5.23 billion, and so Apple seems to have defied potential consumer weakness headwinds. Given the macro environment both in the U.S. and international markets, this revenue beat is one for the ages.

iPhone Stole the Show

The iPhone is, of course, their most important product, and so it is worth a closer look. For Q1, as shown above, they reported sales of $85.269 billion, and that's a 23.33% increase YoY. This is clearly a massive acceleration of growth from Q4's 6.06%, and so it seems that the latest generation of iPhones is resonating strongly with consumers. Note that iPhone was Apple's top-performing category of Q1 and it is always encouraging to see a company's flagship segment outperform. There were some concerns regarding the iPhone's competitiveness in a world of AI-powered devices, but at this point, there are no signs that this has been a major factor.

We'll touch on their overall China sales below, but earlier this month it was reported that iPhone was the only smartphone that grew sales in the market in January. China is known for a very competitive mobile device market, and so the fact that Apple's iPhone is seeing such strong results there is a testament to the attractiveness of their latest generation of devices.

China Was A Major Driver

China has long been an important market for Apple, but in Q1, growth was staggeringly impressive. Of all the geographic markets that Apple serves, China saw the largest percentage revenue increase by a huge margin. In the fiscal first quarter, they reported $25.53 billion in revenues, up 37.9%. The second fastest growing sales region was the Rest of Asia Pacific, with growth of 18%, and so China was a major source of outperformance.

What makes this particularly impressive is the fact that China's Consumer Confidence Index remains subdued. While there has been some improvement, the index remains miles below 2021 levels, and so people there are still overall reluctant to spend. Apple's explosive growth there shows that their value proposition is very strong and that they're clearly doing something very right.

Margins Improve YoY & QoQ

In addition to seeing good business, Apple is making more off of these sales. As you can see in the chart above, gross margin has expanded significantly both YoY and QoQ to show that their pricing power is as strong as ever. With significant competition, especially in China, the ability to expand gross margin is impressive.

While operating margin also improved YoY, it should be noted that the expansion was less than the 128 basis points for gross margin. For operating margin, there was a 91 basis point improvement, and so, there was a bit of operating inefficiency. However, this is not as unfavorable as it seems. If you take a look at their income statement, you can see that R&D expense increased by 32% YoY while SG&A expense increased by just 4%. In my previous article, I highlighted that an increased R&D budget may be beneficial for long-term innovation and shareholder value, and so the overall increase in operating expenses really isn't a concern.

Apple reported a diluted EPS of $2.84 and that represented a nice growth rate of 18%. This outpaced the revenue growth rate as an indication that the gross margin improvement made it all the way to the bottom line. In addition, they beat expectations by $0.17 to show outperformance as well.

Financial Outlook

Looking forward to Q2, Apple expects revenues to grow in the range of 13% to 16%. That implies that a mild deceleration may occur, but overall this is a very respectable growth rate in the grand scheme of things and indicates business will remain good. In fact, they stated that iPhone supply will be constrained, and so demand is not the issue for their flagship product. For gross margin, they are expecting this figure to be in the range of 48% to 49%. This should be an expansion from the year-ago quarter's 47%, and so the pricing power strength we have seen is set to continue. Lastly, for operating expenses, their midpoint projection of $18.55 billion represents a 21% jump. This will outpace the expected top line growth rate, but if it's R&D that's the driver of this, again, I wouldn't mind at all.

AI Efforts

An analysis of a big tech name wouldn't be complete without a discussion of their AI efforts. While Apple has been struggling to keep pace with competitors in this area, they are still trying to remain relevant. Earlier this month it was reported that Apple is planning to update CarPlay so that users can access third-party chatbots. This should help to secure Apple's position in vehicle infotainment.

Furthermore, just days ago a report came out stating that the company is developing AI glasses and a pendant, and also working to update their AirPods with AI features. This is an even clearer statement by the company that they aren't going down in the AI showdown without a fight.

It's not all good news, however, as it was reported earlier in the month that Apple has scaled back plans for an AI-based health coach service. That said, they are still looking to roll out features in their existing Health app over time, and so this is a hiccup rather than a derailment of their AI efforts, in my view.

Memory Shortage Risks

Just today, Evercore stated that Apple has seen bullish sentiment despite the memory shortage potentially affecting the hardware sector. In my view, investors should remain somewhat cautious over this issue. With AI data centers causing unprecedented demand for memory chips, supply is tight and prices are rising. For a company like Apple, whose products need memory chips, this could create some challenges. Therefore, while business activity is seeing good momentum and the demand for their latest iPhone is high, the memory chip shortage still has the potential to upend an otherwise strong year of sales. Investors should keep this in mind.

Valuation Could Expand

Apple's forward P/E ratio is notably off its highs seen in 2025. While a reading of 31.18 isn't particularly cheap, I believe the risk/reward has become net positive for investors now. In this article, it was determined that Q1 was a robust quarter for the company. Revenue growth is at multiyear highs, their latest iPhones are seeing strong performance, and the financial outlook is respectable. Furthermore, the company is continuing to boost R&D and is pushing into AI with multiple device releases incoming.

While the memory shortage is a potential risk to keep in mind, I would say that the strong momentum Apple is seeing more than justifies the valuation, and so there may actually still be room for some multiple expansion. The company hasn't seen this strong of a trajectory for an extended period of time, and with their prospects seemingly strengthening, now may not be a bad time to buy the stock.

Takeaway

As stated in the intro, I maintained my hold rating on Apple in my previous update due to a lack of catalysts. In today's analysis, I believe I found enough bullish signs to justify an upgrade to a buy rating. As stated above, revenue growth has accelerated strongly, with the latest iPhone being a major hit, particularly in China. In addition, the upcoming launches of new AI-powered devices could power growth, and so despite some risks, Apple's strengthening prospects are likely to drive upside in the stock.

data.title: FEPI: Buying On Declines Can Lead To Success
data.description:
Overview

The market indices haven't been off to the greatest start for 2026. As uncertainty within the technology sector rises, the indices have continued to trade sideways. Technology stocks have sold off from their highs, while capital flows into stocks that are perceived as more defensive against the threat of AI. With volatility rising, I believe that REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) may be able to capitalize on this environment and deliver elevated income levels for investors. When I previously covered FEPI, I issued a buy rating due to the potential to collect tax-efficient income through a longer holding period. Since my last coverage, the fund has just scraped by with a positive total return.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FEPI's share price has declined by 16.3%. Although market indices have risen to all-time highs over the last year, the fund continues to demonstrate that it is structurally incapable of fully participating in the upside growth. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits around 6.7% over the same time frame. FEPI now provides investors with a starting dividend yield of ~25%, while issuing payouts on a monthly basis.

After closely observing many different kinds of option ETFs, I believe that investors should have clear expectations on FEPI's performance going forward. The emphasis is clearly put on high-income generation, and this comes with several tradeoffs that must be considered. For instance, FEPI provides diversification across high quality businesses, but the ETF is still prone to NAV erosion through unfavorable market cycles. Additionally, FEPI is almost certain to underperform compared to traditional indices over a longer holding period. Even if technology companies trend sideways over the next few quarters, I am cautious about FEPI's ability to outperform. So let's start by quickly reviewing the underlying strategy that FEPI implements to generate its income.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, FEPI has total assets under management of $582 million. Looking at the fund's history, we can see how FEPI's AUM has steadily risen since the fund's inception. This is a signal that investors continue to have confidence in the underlying strategy, despite the declining share price. Popular alternative option-focused funds have seen their underlying AUM decline in recent months as capital flows out of these higher yielding assets. My assumption is that, like myself, investors are disappointed in their inability to maintain a steady NAV over time.

In order to generate the necessary income levels, FEPI implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy against its selected holdings. FEPI is actively managed so the holdings will consistently change month to month, based on market conditions. Furthermore, FEPI will typically write options that have an expiration date that is a month out. This allows the fund ample time to capture any positive growth of its positions. The fund currently has an expense ratio of 0.65%, which is fair considering the high yield that is offered to investors.

The inclusion of this option strategy means that FEPI can harness the volatility of its positions while also allowing FEPI to participate in a greater amount of capital appreciation. I believe that there's a solid chance that payouts remain at their current range or increase due to the top holdings at this time. Rather than replicate a specific index, FEPI takes a more focused approach to its holdings. As we can see below, Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) is the largest holding at 8.73% of assets. This is followed by many other notable names, including Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Apple Inc. (AAPL), Intel Corporation (INTC), NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), just to name a few. So FEPI essentially makes it possible to collect income from holdings that don't typically offer a meaningful dividend yield.

The latest source of volatility in the markets can be attributed to the sentiment around the massive CapEx spending of the large-cap leaders, which FEPI does provide exposure to. With the estimated growth of AI over the next few years, the leaders with large free cash flows to burn, such as Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), are collectively contributing hundreds of millions of dollars to build out their infrastructure to support this demand. However, investors are questioning whether or not this large level of capex spend is actually worth the elevated costs since there are limited examples of businesses that are currently able to generate higher earnings as a result of the elevated spend.

So why would anyone buy FEPI instead of the underlying stocks it holds? Well, if the rest of 2026 follows the tone laid out so far, the answer is FEPI's ability to harness the volatility of the market. If tech continues to trade sideways as a result of the uncertainty, traditional stocks will see no growth. However, FEPI can still print cash flow in a sideways market, which arguably makes it a hedge against unfavorable conditions. So while the rest of the market waits for a recovery, FEPI investors are collecting cash flow now and getting paid to wait for the reversal. This makes it possible to collect distributions from FEPI and accumulate a reserve, which then makes it possible to deploy a lot more capital into discounted growth positions in the market.

Maintain Realistic Expectations

The primary downside of an option ETF is that the upside growth is capped at whatever the selected strike price is. So if FEPI holds an asset priced at $100 per share, the fund may select a strike price of $110 per share. If that same asset rises to $115 per share because of a strong earnings report, the upside growth above the strike price of $110 is essentially sacrificed in exchange for the option premiums collected.

I want investors to keep this weakness in mind regarding FEPI's outlook. Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market could increase to ~$3.5 trillion by 2033. This would represent a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033. Even if this estimate is half wrong, this would still represent a potential double-digit upside momentum over the next few years. So if you're an investor that is bullish on the outlook of AI, FEPI is certainly not the most efficient way to participate in that growth.

So despite market indices rising to all-time highs over the last year, FEPI isn't structurally capable of delivering the same level of price growth. So when we measure the share price since inception, we can see that FEPI has declined by about 18.8%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 42% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should realistically expect that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time. If FEPI isn't capable of delivering price growth during one of the strongest bull markets over the last decade, we should not expect FEPI to offer any compelling upside growth during milder rallies.

The expectation is that the option premiums collected would be able to generate income that can help the fund outperform during a sideways trend. Over the last three months, the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) has provided a total return of 1.7%. Despite FEPI providing direct exposure to companies that are within the index, the fund has failed to provide a total return that outpaces QQQ. FEPI's total return over the last three months sits at a small loss of 1.4%, which I believe to be an indication that the premiums collected were not actually large enough to support the distributions being paid.

The most ideal scenario would be one where FEPI's share price also trends sideways alongside market indices, but the distributions will allow FEPI to outperform. So while traditional index fund investors are stuck waiting for conditions to recover, FEPI shareholders are collecting cash flow that results in a higher total return and simultaneously provides investors the opportunity to accumulate capital that can be redeployed into discounted opportunities in the market.

However, the recent lag in FEPI's performance may simply be a result of the rapid pullback in recent weeks. As the market eventually stabilizes, FEPI may be able to successfully execute its option strategy and close the performance gap against QQQ. The main issue that I have with the fund is that it is very unlikely for FEPI to recover from market declines. While the option writing strategy caps upside growth, there currently isn't a strategy that actively tries to protect against downside growth.

So if we experience a severe pullback, FEPI will also see its share price rapidly decline. Since options cap upside growth and the fund has already demonstrated that it is incapable of providing any meaningful upside, I anticipate that FEPI's share price will continue to trend downward throughout its lifetime. This is just the nature of these higher yielding funds and investors can still find success with FEPI if they maintain realistic expectations. Since the share price is likely to erode, I believe that it makes the most sense to only accumulate shares during downturns or periods of uncertainty, like we are currently experiencing.

Despite these vulnerabilities, I still believe that this is an attractive buying opportunity. With the tech markets pulling back, I believe this was a brief little breather that helped to reset some valuations. Once the market realizes that the AI hype is justified, I believe positive momentum will eventually continue. For instance, I believe that the next leg of AI growth may possibly come from cost efficiencies. As companies are able to use AI to reduce operating costs and reliance on headcount, we may see a point where companies are capable of reducing their income down to levels that were once unimaginable. We've already seen companies like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) implement several layoffs as they utilize AI to create a more efficient operating process.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.9548 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 25.2%. The fund has typically aimed to maintain an annual payout around 25%, as per its prospectus, but I do worry about the inevitable erosion of distributions over time. As the share price and NAV erode over time, the fund will have fewer assets that it can actively deploy for its strategy. Lower assets translate to lower option premiums, which will eventually lead to reduced payouts to help offset the share price deterioration. If FEPI chooses not to reduce payouts, this will increase the speed at which the share price erodes, since the fund may frequently find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earns.

Furthermore, NAV erosion can become more relevant during market declines. I the underlying stocks all, FEPI will not be able to generate as much option income. Writing a 5% to 10% OTM option on a $50 stock will generate much less income compared to the same OTM call written on a $100 stock. So if we see the stocks within FEPI decline and fail to recover, this can be disastrous if experienced over an extended period of time.

When markets rapidly pulled back in April of 2025, we can see below how FEPI's payouts were also reduced. Similarly, the latest payout for February was also on the lower end of its typical range, which can be attributed to the recent pullback in tech. Although the fund has done fine keeping payouts within a consistent range so far, this can grow to become a larger issue if we ever experience a prolonged pullback. Market conditions similar to 2022 may cause FEPI to experience severe NAV erosion, so caution is advised. Therefore, investors need to understand that FEPI's higher dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to better resilience during market pullbacks.

The only scenario where NAV growth is likely, is one where the tech markets slowly creep higher over time. Marginal increases to the share price allow FEPI's OTM strategy to better capture upside growth, unlike the rapid price growth we've seen over the last few quarters. Therefore, this environment of skepticism around the growth of AI may actually be beneficial for FEPI's growth since appreciation may occur much more slowly.

In more positive news, FEPI still prioritizes tax-efficient distributions. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the payouts on a YTD basis were classified as return of capital. Return of capital distributions are favorable since they aren't classified as income and, therefore, aren't taxed as such. The only downside is that return of capital distributions have a direct impact on the NAV and share price. So if FEPI issues a $1 distribution, the share price will fall by $1.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on FEPI but I want investors to maintain realistic expectations. The fund's upside growth is severely limited, and the fund's inability to capture meaningful price growth through one of the strongest bull markets should be an indication of that. However, the fund is sacrificing the upside growth in exchange for income generation. If technology growth slows, this may actually present a more favorable environment for FEPI since it will allow the OTM option strategy to capture greater upside. While traditional stock investors are stuck waiting for sentiment to improve, FEPI investors are collecting cash flow now. Despite the risks, FEPI continues to deliver tax-efficient distributions and can be a great way to generate income from some of the highest quality names in the technology sector.

data.title: Planet Fitness: Too Heavy Debt And Valuation
data.description:
Intro

Before we do the heavy lifting on the fundamentals, a brief overview of one of the largest fitness chains on the planet.

Planet Fitness (PLNT) is not only one of the largest, but also the fastest-growing franchisors and operators of fitness clubs. The company will soon have 3,000+ studios (2,896 per the latest update) with around 21 million members. PLNT owns and self-operates ~10% of the clubs. The remainder is franchised and third-party managed.

Long-term, shares have been on the rise, being up almost 400% since being publicly listed.

Besides the healthier-living lifestyle adoption, especially the weight-loss wave seems to be a meaningful tailwind for the business. People who take the prominent weight-loss medications need to take care of their muscle mass—be it at companies like Planet Fitness.

Even though I like this thesis, I do not like the stock at the current setup. It is too expensive in my view, while the debt load adds more to my concerns.

The stock does not look that fit

Planet Fitness has strong recognition value. The colors purple and yellow, as well as its self-proclaimed mission to welcome everyone free of judgment, regardless of any attributes and fitness level, define the company.

The entry level membership starts at $15/month, offering a low entry hurdle for a membership sign-up. More ambitious gym visitors can choose a black card (to be raised from 24.99 USD to 29.99 USD), offering further add-ons, among others massage chairs, exclusive training areas, etc.

A bit surprisingly for me, PLNT doesn’t offer (from their perspective) non-essentials such as group exercise classes, pools, or juice bars. The reason is they focus on using available space as efficiently as possible. I said, surprisingly for me, because here in Germany—not necessarily the benchmark, but that’s what I have in front of me to compare—even lower-cost gyms do offer at least some sort of exercise classes. Even if only with a monitor, this form of training has risen in popularity, among others due to its motivational factor (think of “together we are strong”).

Be it, Planet Fitness should turn more and more into a less capital-intensive business. Franchising is known for being high margin, as the franchisee is responsible for operating the business—including day-to-day costs. For around 90% of their branded studios, they collect royalties for licensing. A quick look at Capex reveals that it has been broadly stable over the recent quarters—despite the company being in heavy expansion mode, as can be seen by rising sales.

Management is currently not paying dividends, but investing in the business, while free cash flow is used on an opportunistic basis to repurchase shares.

There were even a few quarters where management spent more on buybacks than was generated in FCF. Longer-time readers of my analyses know that I am not a big fan of such moves. Especially if the balance sheet is strongly leveraged while shares are priced at a high multiple.

Let’s check whether this is the case. Indeed, net debt is around $1.7 billion. Compared to FCF of ~$200 million, we are talking about a leverage factor of more than 8. The more commonly used net debt to EBITDA metric shows 3.3x, which sounds significantly lower, but even this figure is not low.

So PLNT, for my taste, operates with too much leverage. And this is real financial debt, without leasing.

Regarding the high-multiple question, unfortunately this, too, is the case. PLNT has an equity market cap of $7.6 billion for an enterprise value of around $9.3 billion. Compared to FCF, we are talking about more than 45x.

On a price-to-earnings basis, PLNT stock is valued at 30x. There’s a noticeable gap between net income and FCF, but whichever figure we use, both are high multiples, having priced in solid growth.

So, let’s discuss growth to get a better feel for the static multiples from above.

On their recently held Investor Day, Planet Fitness showed a few interesting slides in their presentation. The total gym market as a whole is barely growing (total U.S. gym membership growth of 1% since 2019), which might be a bit surprising, given the weight-loss tailwinds.

PLNT is focusing on the “affordable mid-market” segment, so to speak (HVLP = high value, low price), benefiting and even growing market share.

What this also tells us is that cost-of-living struggles continue. Else mid-tier and above classified gyms would not be coping with shrinking demand. For me, this is a clear sign of down-trading and value-seeking behavior. In other words, this carries the risk of a reversal sometime, as these potentially “borrowed” customers someday might want to return to higher-value offers like pools or training classes.

That’s why I am a bit cautious about the current growth story. It looks more like a redistribution than a full-blown growth story when looking at the sector.

After the lockdown years, sales growth almost collapsed into only low-single digits. For a bit more than a year, sales growth dynamics have accelerated again for Planet Fitness, likely as a result of what I described above. In a market that is barely growing, we should be more cautious about extrapolating such strong figures into the future.

However, the market is expecting exactly that. PLNT stock is priced for growth in a practically not-growing market. If we subtract inflation from the tiny 1% segment growth, real growth is negative. Despite weight-loss dynamics.

This could change, of course, but I would not ignore the cost component.

I rate PLNT a sell, as the stock is too richly valued, waiting for a better entry point.

The upcoming FY25 result on 24 February 2026 will likely not bring any big surprises regarding the closed fiscal year 2025. But I’d look closely at the guidance (growth momentum) and, if commented on, the Q1 26 dynamic, especially regarding the price hike for the Black Card (25% more expensive). Analysts could ask on the call about churn and pricing sensitivity.

Risks to My Thesis

The weight-loss theme could produce enduring demand for gyms, especially if combined with low prices but high value, facing the ongoing cost of living struggles.

Planet Fitness, with a market cap of less than $8 billion, could be acquired, invalidating my sell rating.

If management is able to keep pace, expanding strongly while delivering mid-double-digit sales growth, the current valuation might be less expensive than I am viewing it.

Conclusion

PLNT Fitness looks like a promising idea to play the weight-loss theme that maybe not everyone has on their radar (focus is on the drugs). However, high debt and valuation multiples force them to stay sidelined.

data.title: PayPal's Q4 Shock: Collapse Narrative Vs. Earnings Reality
data.description:
All the bad news possible in PayPal Holdings (PYPL) seems to be already in, after a torrid Q4. Leadership changes, a sharp slowdown in branded checkout growth, lower near-term guidance, and the withdrawal of prior long-term targets have led to a sharp decline in share prices. It does look like there is no room for further negatives. With shares now trading at 7-8x forward earnings, a classic contrarian opportunity emerges - expectations reset, valuation compressed, and a turnaround speculation probabilistically more supported.

However, this contrarian setup could be quite crowded, as value picks with turnaround potential are rare in today's growth markets, especially for a quality franchise like PayPal. So, it is important to argue from the other side to see whether what looks obviously cheap indeed comes with a genuine turnaround merit or whether that contrarian position simply assumes mean reversion because the stock has fallen enough.

In this thesis I do not assume that because a slower or flat earnings growth recalibration looks like expectations have been reset aggressively, it is necessarily a bottom. The thesis looks at whether current assumptions have decisive strength in expecting an EPS bottom here, and what the implications of further contractions and their odds are.

Long-term take rate durability (the percentage PayPal keeps from every transaction), volumes, and earnings transmission are all not immune from further fall at today's level. However, I find it harder work to justify a further decline than stabilization (if not a rebound). And that conservative base case of an absence of growth is already in the valuations today. So, overall, the downside risks appear lower in magnitude and odds than a turnaround from here. A measured Buy in PayPal appears to be more fundamentally supported on the basis of evidence available.

What Negatives Have Emerged?

Before analyzing the impact of the negatives, let us first understand the observable and implied problems that plague PayPal - incremental pressures that have emerged from Q4 2025 alone. I found only three real measurable negatives. One, branded checkout was reported as materially down from 5% to 1% - a significant 4 percentage point decline, pushing it to almost flat territory. The second factor is a slower exit and a lower guide into 2026. Branded checkout is guided as only slightly positive to low single digits. A 3 point transaction margin impact from investments is also expected, supporting a flat to slightly negative EPS outlook. The third negative development is a withdrawal of the multi-year long term targets shared earlier, signaling reduced visibility.

What Q4 Numbers Show - Take Rate Base Case

The impact of investments is reflected in numbers reported in Q4 (although this could be an open area to monitor), and visibility is a long duration problem to be solved in the valuation section. The remaining and only operational negative already at play is the branded checkout slowdown. What impact has it had so far? In take rate terms, the 1.65% number reported in Q4 is 9 bps lower YoY. That may seem to be a significant impact and potentially attributable to the branded checkout slowdown, but not conclusively so, if we dig a little deeper. Q4 declines reported YoY mask sequential stability in take rates - in fact, nothing declined materially in the whole of 2025. The YoY negative emerges from a stiffer comp from an outlier 4Q 2024.

In other words, the impact of branded checkout has not led to material pressures on the take rate yet. Further worsening in the mix or a higher incentive environment is required to hurt take rates ahead. Here is the problem with arguing in favor of such a decline. One, the guidance has already accepted a reset ahead and still claimed positive to low single-digit branded checkout growth in 2026. Even if it remains flat, the larger drop from 5% to 1% has had no significant effect, so further deterioration is difficult to accept as a base case.

A meaningful compression from here needs to see a permanent and material pricing reset due to incentives, or merchant leverage forcing structural fee erosion, or a further worsening of the mix. The incentives are expected to be tactical and performance-linked, and their impact on the take rate was not a huge net drag in Q4. Nothing yet indicates a permanent fee reduction, although the need to secure placements to drive engagement does show competitive pressures that should be watched. On the possibility of mix worsening, PSP and Venmo monetization trends are encouraging - areas where yields have been traditionally lower.

Actual evidence from Q4 does not indicate a trajectory that worsens fee erosion. On the contrary, PayPal's differentiated conversion data (95% vaulted success) still looks supportive of pricing power. Biometrics adoption has seen 2-5 points of conversion gains. Merchants who implemented PayPal's latest checkout features were reported to be growing at double digits.

Volumes, Revenue, EPS Implications

While branded checkout impact was a drag, there is no TPV impact noted in Q4 at all. A 6% FX-neutral growth is as good as we have seen in the past 6 quarters, if not better than most. Q4 did see strong volumes in Venmo, BNPL, PSP, Enterprise, and Tap to Pay. So, overall, the branded checkout impact is contained, and volume strength can still compensate for a lot of take rate compression if that happens ahead, while keeping revenue growth still positive. The revenue transmission trails volume growth, but that is not a new phenomenon - this can be attributed to the mix changes that have been at play throughout 2025. Q4 numbers do show a moderation in revenue transmission from volume, but nothing structurally indicates a collapse, and there is not sufficient reason yet to be able to confidently model a lower revenue transmission ahead. This could be a watchlist item at best.

Finally, the EPS trajectory does show a visible decline from Q2 2025, but still remains positive in terms of YoY growth, so it is indeed a trend that needs to be watched, but has not collapsed to historical lows (like its valuations have). There are two routes open for further deterioration in EPS ahead. One, a larger-than-expected 3-point TM headwind (assuming Q4 effects from these headwinds were absent, which is not the case). And two, worsening in branded checkout, which we have discussed as looking like a controlled risk at the moment.

Valuations, Visibility, and Residual Risks

We started the thesis with the negatives at play that have emerged in Q4. After analyzing the take rate and volume, EPS transmission factors, I am left with two residual risks that need to be monitored: not enough data to indicate a base case of further decline. Those two risks are competitive pressures (and value creation at checkout) and ongoing deterioration in revenue and EPS transmission (including impact from investments).

The negative we haven't addressed yet is lower visibility (due to far year targets withdrawal). Here, the immediate next year's guidance does not point to anything severely crushing that jumps out in 2026. In fact, EPS outlook is a low single-digit decline to slightly positive. And this poor visibility or a slower EPS road ahead is already priced in - Seeking Alpha analyst estimates show a flat 2026 and only ~10% EPS growth in 2027. PayPal has to deliver a declining EPS regime in 2026 and a flat 2027 outlook to really open up further downside risks, because the valuation could still absorb flat to low single-digit negative EPS growth territory, having corrected meaningfully from the 2025 highs (almost ~50% from the peak valuations).

Valuations have corrected by over 20% just after Q4. The EPS revisions in the past one month (which includes the post-Q4 effect) have been mid- to high single-digit down from earlier estimates. While that is a material downgrade, the forward PE ratio has shrunk by a far larger degree, indicating that the share price has reacted more severely than the forward year earnings downgrade.

That correction could still be fair, because outer-year and long-duration growth revisions also show a cascading effect that reduces terminal value. This could be a result of poorer visibility ahead, or could be a mispriced overreaction. The only case that is difficult to assign high odds to is that earnings will deteriorate further from the expectation reset we had in Q4 (which is what emerges from my analysis thus far). And even if that happens, valuations currently could support some earnings shocks if the structural competitive advantages at checkout and the turnaround initiatives demonstrate credible long-term durability from here.

So, overall, based on current resilience and data (Q4), I think PayPal can be considered a contrarian buy with risks to monitor. The downside risks appear far lower, while a successful phase of investment-driven gains could indeed open up significant upside from a valuation rebound, plus the earnings improvement.

data.title: CEF Market Review: The Double-Whammy Of 'Juicy Yield' CEFs
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual fund news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of February. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferred/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was a good week for CEFs as NAVs were higher across all sectors. MLPs and Utilities finished ahead. Discounts were mixed.

The median CEF sector discount has tightened to around 2% below its longer-term average level, which marks the sector as fairly expensive for now.

Market Themes

John Hancock CEFs released quarterly financials. These mostly preferred funds HPI, HPF and HPS tend to trade at a premium to the sector because of their high distribution rates.

Their NAV distribution rates are around 2% above the sector median levels. However, the financials confirm that these high distribution rates are not backed up by income. They have low distribution coverage of sub-80%.

The funds underperformed the sector over the past year, in part because of underperformance in total NAV returns as well as high starting valuations, which remain high. 3-year valuation-adjusted total NAV returns are the lowest in the sector apart from NPFD. The funds have a large minority corporate bond allocation, so they are less compelling as preferred assets or from a qualified-tax perspective.

They also highlight the double whammy with chasing "juicy yield" CEFs with no regard to income. Because these funds tend to trade at more expensive valuations, investors are actually earning less in net income than they would in a similar fund with a lower distribution rate. And two, a high starting valuation limits upside in the fund and increases the downside in case of a distribution rightsizing.

Market Commentary

CLO equity NAVs are starting to come out for January. ECC was down 3.7%. EIC was down very marginally for a 0.7% total NAV return in January. EIC is the only fund in the sector that generated a positive total NAV return last year. It was not all that exciting; however, the fund got punished just as much as the rest of the suite. In short, the market is not differentiating EIC from the rest of the sector despite its significantly lower CLO equity allocation, which means it remains an opportunity. EIC trades at a 22% discount.

TCW Strategic Income Fund (TSI) announced a rights offering. The offering will be 1:3 with the record date being 19-Feb, expiring on 18-Mar. The formula will be a 7.5% discount to the price, floored at a 12% discount to NAV. The rights are transferable and will start to trade on 18 Feb under the ticker TSI RT.

TSI also announced it raised the distribution by 31% to $0.283. The latest report shows it earns around $0.025 in NII. The jump in the distribution was likely intended to prevent an excessive drop in the price on the announcement of a rights offering. The stock fell around 5% at the open yesterday, though it clawed back around half of this, leaving the fund down 2.3% on the day. TSI is an unusual fund with a mostly MBS allocation and no leverage. Performance has been unexciting.

BlackRock completed the reorganization of some of its Muni CEFs (BFZ) into MUC, BNY, MHN into MYN and MUE, BFK, BYM, BLE into MHD). The surviving funds will adopt a discount management program. Specifically, if they trade at an average discount of more than 10% during a 9-month period, they will conduct a tender offer of 5% of shares at a 2% discount. The measurement period will run to 30 Sep. That's a disappointingly long measurement period. Previously these periods ran for 3 months for BlackRock CEFs. Plus, the discount trigger used to be 7.5% rather than 10%. Now that the surviving funds are significantly larger, they are less vulnerable to an activist takeover, so BlackRock doesn't have to try as hard to be shareholder friendly.

data.title: GRAIL: A Major Trial Disappointment
data.description:
GRAIL, Inc. (GRAL) reported the extensive NHS-Galleri trial’s results alongside the company’s Q4 results. The trial results weren’t as good as was hoped; the stock has reacted very strongly due to the lack of statistically significant findings of clinical utility, being down by -50% following the study results as of writing. The trial came with major importance, in my opinion making a strong stock reaction justified.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous December 2025 article on the stock, titled “GRAIL: Making Strides In Cancer Detection.” The stock has since lost --43% of its value. The S&P 500 has lost -1% in the same period.

NHS-Galleri Results Were a Disappointment

GRAIL announced topline results of the extensive three-year NHS-Galleri trial, which used a total of 142 thousand participants aged 50 to 77. The U.K. trial is the largest, and only randomized controlled trial of any MCED test, making its results very important. Unfortunately, the trial’s key findings didn’t meet expectations. After PATHFINDER 2 previously found good results for Galleri, as I wrote in my previous December article, NHS-Galleri now suggests weaker conclusions of current clinical utility for Galleri with a multiple times larger participant group and a highly valuable randomized controlled trial setting.

The key headline is that the trial did not observe a statistically significant reduction in Stage III-IV cancers among participants. This key headline is critical regarding clinical utility—not finding statistically significant evidence is a substantial disappointment in such an extensive clinical trial, especially as Galleri’s PATHFINDER 2 study set quite good expectations for Galleri's predictive value. GRAIL’s stock’s previous run-up, and crash following the NHS-Galleri topline results, underline the market’s disappointment.

GRAIL plans to extend the trial’s follow-up period by 6-12 months. A longer follow-up period could improve study results’ reliability as data matures, but for now, initial topline results are weaker than was hoped.

The trial did find a favorable trend towards fewer Stage III-IV cancers in 12 different cancers, a similar positive predictive value “PPV” to GRAIL’s North American studies, and some other positive results in cancer detection, according to GRAIL’s press release. Overall results were still disappointingly inconclusive due to the lack of statistical significance in the most important endpoint.

Ultimately, the key question is how significant the trial results are for GRAIL’s commercialization. The stock’s sharp reaction suggests that the implications are massive.

I also believe that the NHS-Galleri trial holds very material importance for GRAIL. The NHS-Galleri trial was set to explore whether Galleri could be adopted for a national cancer screening program within the NHS. A national healthcare program with the U.K.’s nearly 70 million population could create massive revenue potential for GRAIL depending on the program’s patient reach. I believe that inconclusive trial results make an extensive commercial program with the NHS much more unlikely.

Implications reach past the U.K. as well. GRAIL completed its application for Galleri’s FDA premarket approval in January. The approval process uses data from PATHFINDER 2, but also from first-year data from the NHS-Galleri trial. FDA approval will more than likely be critical to gain commercial mass in the U.S. market, and NHS-Galleri’s failure to meet the primary endpoint could affect approval. The review process is anticipated by GRAIL to take around 12 months.

Fundamentally, the inconclusive trial results from the most extensive clinical trial on MCEDs make GRAIL’s value proposition more uncertain. Galleri is much more unlikely to gain insurance coverage with uncertain proof of clinical utility. Additional trials will need to come up with more conclusive results to prove clinical utility; the focus shifts towards GRAIL's REACH study, aiming to study 50 thousand participants in the Medicare population.

While the key endpoint result of NHS-Galleri is clearly worse than anticipated, I believe that the trial results don’t deteriorate the whole Galleri story. The study did still show some positive results regarding clinical utility. A prolonged follow-up period could strengthen the trial’s findings. More detailed NHS-Galleri trial results are going to be presented in mid-2026. For now, the results do make the overall Galleri story much more uncertain, justifying a sharp stock reaction.

GRAIL’s Financials Performed as Expected in Q4

GRAIL's Q4 financials were close to expectations. Revenues came in at $43.6 million, up by 14% year-on-year. The Galleri test’s revenues improved by 31% to $41.3 million, being the most important revenue stream to watch. The adjusted EBITDA loss came in at -$71.8 million, consistent with previous quarters in 2025 but improving by $12.2 million year-on-year. GRAIL is still largely in the development phase; the revenue base is way too small to generate positive earnings yet.

Revenues were very close to Wall Street's consensus, and the quarter’s EPS of -$2.44 even beat Wall Street’s expectations by $0.22.

GRAIL’s partnership with Hims & Hers (HIMS) among other commercial expansions, creates revenue momentum for 2026 as well. GRAIL guides Galleri to show sales growth of 22-32% for the year, and that the company’s cash burn will be no more than $300 million. GRAIL ended the quarter with $904 million in combined cash and short-term marketable securities, enabling the company to burn cash for the next few years.

Ultimately, while growth momentum is positive, the focus remains on FDA approval and other meaningful regulatory progress that would enable fast commercial adoption. Current financials don’t have very much significance in my opinion past the cash burn rate.

Valuation

Using my previous assumptions of $900 million in operating expenses upon maturation, a 55% gross margin, and a justifiable mature P/E of 20, and adding a 25% tax rate, GRAIL’s stock price of $50.6 as of writing implies revenues of around $1.88 billion upon maturation. GRAIL’s revenue potential is multiple times larger if clinical utility is proven extensively, but even reaching such a revenue figure is still on uncertain grounds.

For now, I believe that such a valuation doesn’t seem too far off from a fair base valuation that incorporates multiple potential financial scenarios.

The valuation remains very volatile; a wide range of stock prices can be justified with such calculations. The calculation reflects a number of highly volatile assumptions regarding operating expenses, revenues, and earnings multiples, and only works as a very rough illustration of potential stock pricing.

Takeaway

NHS-Galleri results weren’t satisfactory, as GRAIL didn’t prove the main endpoint with statistical significance. The trial results question Galleri’s clinical utility, weaken prospects for an extensive national program with the NHS, and cast a slight shadow on the FDA approval process; a negative stock reaction was justified. The investment case is now much more uncertain, but ultimately, I don’t expect the trial results to deteriorate the whole Galleri story. I remain at a Hold rating.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - February 2026
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. The regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. The emphasis here is on high growth rather than high yield. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who Should Follow This Series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace than in their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook

Overall, the market appears to be somewhat tired and stalled. Even though the market had a reasonably good start in the new year, with the S&P 500 advancing 1.4% in the month of January. We have already seen the S&P 500 make new all-time highs four times this year, and it also briefly crossed the psychologically important level of SPX 7000 but retreated quickly afterward.

During this consolidation phase, some new trends that started in 2025 are picking up steam. For example, the foreign equities are outperforming the U.S. equities, which started in 2025. Also, the mid-cap and small-cap stocks are expected to outperform the large-cap stocks.

The big question is if the AI rally of the past year is in bubble territory and ready to burst. It is hard to predict, and even if it was, bubbles can last longer than most predictions. That said, there is a growing concern about overspending and over-borrowing by the mega technology companies. With this backdrop, as investors, we need to be cautious.

Another factor that is going to impact the markets and the economy is the Fed's actions on interest rates in 2026. It will be pivotal in shaping the broader economy and the market. The Fed stayed pat and kept the interest rates on hold (target rate between 3.50% and 3.75%) during their most recent FOMC meeting on 27/28th January. However, with the most recent inflation numbers, there are now higher expectations of a rate cut in June 2026.

You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatch Tool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

Note: For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article). This section is common among articles under this series. The regular readers could skip this section.

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio (dividend per share divided by EPS)
  • Past three years and five-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/capital and debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or revenue growth during the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks
